title: Errors, throw and recovery boundaries

publication.changeSummary: Technology-specific JavaScript: Language and Browser Applications draft for Errors, throw and recovery boundaries; independent standards, browser, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Exceptions represent exceptional control transfer, and error handling should preserve cause, context, cleanup, and user-safe messaging.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A parser throws a typed error with cause and the UI converts it into a bounded message while logs retain safe context. Your goal is to design and test error behavior across domain, network and presentation modules. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Browser state and security; Dependency boundaries; Sources, console and debugger; Promises and chains. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Technology rule
sections.body: Exceptions represent exceptional control transfer, and error handling should preserve cause, context, cleanup, and user-safe messaging. Apply this rule to the actual markup, stylesheet, JavaScript, or component state rather than memorizing its name.

sections.heading: Boundary and browser reality
sections.body: Catching too broadly hides defects, finally can override outcomes, and rejected promises require asynchronous handling. This draft therefore rejects: An empty catch block makes an operation robust by preventing crashes.

includes: Create meaningful errors, preserve causes and recover only where a user or caller can act.
Design and test error behavior across domain, network and presentation modules.
excludes: Catching too broadly hides defects, finally can override outcomes, and rejected promises require asynchronous handling.

outcomes: Design and test error behavior across domain, network and presentation modules.

examples.title: Errors, throw and recovery boundaries: concrete case
examples.situation: A parser throws a typed error with cause and the UI converts it into a bounded message while logs retain safe context.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the exact artifact and starting state: A parser throws a typed error with cause and the UI converts it into a bounded message while logs retain safe context.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the correction: Catch only where recovery or context is possible; do not silently swallow errors.
examples.result: The result follows because Catch only where recovery or context is possible; do not silently swallow errors.

examples.title: Errors, throw and recovery boundaries: boundary case
examples.situation: Catching too broadly hides defects, finally can override outcomes, and rejected promises require asynchronous handling.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Start from the documented boundary: Catching too broadly hides defects, finally can override outcomes, and rejected promises require asynchronous handling.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Reject the misconception: An empty catch block makes an operation robust by preventing crashes.
examples.result: The boundary requires this correction: Catch only where recovery or context is possible; do not silently swallow errors.

analogy.example: A coordinated event desk can model Errors, throw and recovery boundaries, with values, tasks, callbacks, and external messages changing observable state.
usefulFor: Building a first mental model for Errors, throw and recovery boundaries before using exact platform terms
limitations: Human coordination does not reproduce coercion, lexical scope, microtasks, prototypes, or browser security boundaries.
